_note: Top 3 physiological roles for each nutrient. Intended for display in the NutrientFinder mobile app info box.

203.name: Protein
roles: Builds and repairs muscle, skin, organs, and connective tissue
Forms enzymes that catalyze virtually every biochemical reaction in the body
Antibodies (immune proteins) defend the body against pathogens and infections

204.name: Total Fat
roles: The brain is ~60% fat; essential fatty acids are critical for cognition and mood
Steroid hormones (estrogen, testosterone, cortisol) are synthesized from dietary fats
Fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K require dietary fat for intestinal absorption

205.name: Carbohydrate
roles: Primary energy source; glucose from carbs is the body's most efficient fuel
The brain exclusively uses glucose for fuel under normal conditions
Complex carbs provide dietary fiber that feeds gut bacteria and supports bowel regularity

208.name: Energy (Calories)
roles: Fuels all cellular processes from heartbeat to brain activity
Supports basal metabolic rate — the energy needed just to sustain life at rest
Powers physical performance, endurance, and post-exercise recovery

269.name: Total Sugars
roles: Rapidly converted to glucose, providing fast energy for muscles and brain
Glucose from sugars is the brain's preferred and exclusive fuel source
Natural sugars in fruit and dairy accompany fiber, vitamins, and minerals

291.name: Dietary Fiber
roles: Feeds beneficial gut bacteria (prebiotics), promoting a healthy microbiome
Slows glucose absorption, preventing blood sugar spikes after meals
Soluble fiber binds bile cholesterol in the gut, reducing LDL (bad) cholesterol

301.name: Calcium
roles: 99% stored in bones and teeth, providing structural hardness and strength
Triggers muscle fiber contraction — required for every movement including heartbeats
Regulates neurotransmitter release at nerve synapses, enabling nervous system signaling

303.name: Iron
roles: Core of hemoglobin in red blood cells; carries oxygen from lungs to every tissue
Required by mitochondria to produce ATP, the universal cellular energy molecule
Supports immune cell proliferation and maturation to fight infections

304.name: Magnesium
roles: Cofactor for 300+ enzymes involved in protein synthesis, DNA replication, and energy production
Regulates electrical activity in nerves and muscles, including the heart
Improves insulin sensitivity and helps regulate blood glucose metabolism

305.name: Phosphorus
roles: Combines with calcium to form hydroxyapatite — the mineral matrix of bones and teeth
Component of ATP (adenosine triphosphate) — the universal energy currency of all cells
A key structural component of phospholipid cell membranes throughout the body

306.name: Potassium
roles: Counteracts sodium's effect, relaxing blood vessels and lowering blood pressure
Works with sodium to regulate muscle contractions, including the heart muscle
The primary intracellular cation, maintaining proper fluid balance inside cells

307.name: Sodium
roles: Regulates fluid distribution between blood and cells, maintaining blood pressure and volume
Sodium ions flowing into nerve cells generate the electrical signals for nerve transmission
Required for muscle contraction; works in opposition to potassium in muscle cells

309.name: Zinc
roles: Activates T-cells and other immune cells that defend against bacteria and viruses
Stimulates skin cell growth and collagen production for wound closure and repair
Cofactor for hundreds of enzymes involved in protein synthesis and DNA production

312.name: Copper
roles: Ceruloplasmin (a copper protein) is essential for mobilizing iron from storage to blood
Component of superoxide dismutase — one of the body's primary antioxidant enzymes
Required for cross-linking collagen and elastin, giving connective tissue strength

315.name: Manganese
roles: Activates enzymes critical for bone matrix protein synthesis and bone mineralization
Cofactor for enzymes that metabolize carbohydrates, amino acids, and cholesterol
Component of manganese superoxide dismutase — the main mitochondrial antioxidant enzyme

317.name: Selenium
roles: Essential for glutathione peroxidase — a powerful enzyme that neutralizes hydrogen peroxide
Required for converting inactive thyroid hormone T4 to the active form T3
Supports proliferation and function of T-cells and natural killer immune cells

318.name: Vitamin A (IU)
roles: Retinal (a form of vitamin A) is the light-sensing molecule in the eye's rod cells
Maintains epithelial integrity throughout the body — a first-line barrier against pathogens
Regulates differentiation and reproduction of skin, gut, lung, and other epithelial cells

320.name: Vitamin A (RAE)
roles: Retinal (derived from vitamin A RAE) is the chromophore in rhodopsin for night vision
Supports innate immunity by maintaining mucous membrane barriers throughout the body
Regulates gene expression for cell growth, differentiation, and programmed cell death

323.name: Vitamin E
roles: Breaks free radical chain reactions in cell membranes, protecting polyunsaturated fats from oxidation
Enhances immune cell production and helps regulate inflammatory and anti-tumor responses
Protects skin cells from UV-induced and environmental oxidative damage

324.name: Vitamin D
roles: Activates calcium-binding proteins in the gut that absorb calcium from food
Regulates calcium and phosphorus deposition in bone, maintaining bone density
Vitamin D receptors on immune cells modulate inflammatory and pathogen-defense responses

328.name: Vitamin D (D2+D3)
roles: Activates intestinal calcium-binding proteins to optimize calcium absorption
Stimulates osteoblasts and modulates osteoclasts to maintain bone mineral density
Regulates over 200 genes involved in immune function, cell growth, and inflammation

401.name: Vitamin C
roles: Donates electrons to neutralize free radicals, protecting DNA, proteins, and lipids
Essential cofactor for prolyl hydroxylase — the enzyme that stabilizes collagen structure
Stimulates production and chemotaxis of neutrophils, lymphocytes, and phagocytes

404.name: Thiamin (B1)
roles: As thiamine pyrophosphate, drives the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA for energy
Required for myelin sheath production and electrical signal transmission in nerve axons
A key coenzyme in the pentose phosphate pathway that produces nucleotides and NADPH

405.name: Riboflavin (B2)
roles: Component of FAD and FMN coenzymes that accept electrons in the mitochondrial energy chain
Required for cellular growth, function, and reproduction throughout the body
Regenerates glutathione from its oxidized form, maintaining intracellular antioxidant defenses

406.name: Niacin (B3)
roles: Precursor to NAD+ — the most critical electron carrier in mitochondrial ATP production
NAD+ is an essential cofactor for DNA repair enzymes (PARPs) that maintain genome integrity
Supports healthy skin barrier function; deficiency causes the dermatitis of pellagra

410.name: Pantothenic Acid (B5)
roles: Component of Coenzyme A (CoA), the central hub of carbohydrate, fat, and protein catabolism
Required for synthesis of steroid hormones, vitamin D, and the neurotransmitter acetylcholine
Supports fatty acid synthesis for new cell membranes during wound healing and growth

415.name: Vitamin B6
roles: Cofactor for 100+ enzymes involved in building and breaking down amino acids
Required to produce serotonin, dopamine, GABA, and norepinephrine from amino acid precursors
Helps produce antibodies and maintains lymphocyte function for adaptive immunity

417.name: Folate
roles: Provides one-carbon units for synthesizing thymidylate — a building block of DNA
Critical during rapid cell division (pregnancy, infancy) for proper tissue formation
Adequate intake in early pregnancy prevents neural tube defects in developing embryos

418.name: Vitamin B12
roles: Required for normal red blood cell maturation; deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia
Maintains the myelin sheath surrounding nerve axons for proper electrical conduction
Works with folate in one-carbon metabolism to produce thymidylate for DNA synthesis

421.name: Choline
roles: Precursor to acetylcholine — the neurotransmitter governing memory, learning, and muscle control
Required to export triglycerides as VLDL particles; prevents fatty liver accumulation
Phosphatidylcholine (from choline) is the dominant phospholipid in every cell membrane

430.name: Vitamin K
roles: Essential cofactor for gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X in the liver
Activates osteocalcin — the protein that anchors calcium ions into the bone mineral matrix
Activates Matrix Gla Protein (MGP) which prevents calcification of arterial walls

435.name: Folate DFE
roles: Provides one-carbon units essential for de novo synthesis of purines and thymidylate
Critical during rapid cell division for DNA integrity and proper gene expression
Adequate periconceptional intake prevents up to 70% of neural tube defect cases

601.name: Cholesterol
roles: Structural precursor for all steroid hormones: cortisol, estrogen, testosterone, and aldosterone
In the skin, cholesterol is converted to vitamin D3 upon UV-B light exposure
Modulates cell membrane fluidity and organizes lipid rafts that coordinate cell signaling

606.name: Saturated Fat
roles: A dense, stable energy source stored in adipose tissue and released between meals
Palmitic and stearic acids are precursors to longer-chain fats used in hormone synthesis
Provides structural rigidity to cell membranes, influencing receptor and ion channel function

645.name: Monounsaturated Fat
roles: Replacing saturated fat with monounsaturated fat (oleic acid) lowers LDL without reducing HDL
Improves insulin sensitivity and helps stabilize post-meal blood glucose responses
Oleic acid reduces inflammatory markers including C-reactive protein and interleukin-6

646.name: Polyunsaturated Fat
roles: DHA (omega-3 PUFA) comprises ~25% of brain fat and is critical for cognition and memory
Omega-3 fatty acids produce resolvins and protectins that actively resolve inflammation
Omega-3s lower triglycerides and reduce cardiac arrhythmia risk by modulating ion channels

221.name: Alcohol (Ethanol)
roles: Provides 7 kcal/gram; metabolized preferentially before fat, carbohydrates, or protein
At low doses causes vasodilation (widening of blood vessels) and a temporary drop in blood pressure
Inhibits glutamate receptors and potentiates GABA receptors, producing central nervous system sedation
